author: @British19
text: I guess the fractional reserve lending  that all banking is based on is not a ponzi scheme 🤔 . Fiat money that is not backed by a fungible  asset is open to abuse ( I.e printing out of thin air ) thus the state of affairs we now find ourselves  in . Crypto currency / digital assets are and will be systemic throughout  the new burgeoning financial system .  Look at the BRICS country's if you want to a glimpse of what's coming, amongst many other new concepts of finance ( stable coins such as USDC ) . Much appreciate your engendering expertise. 👍👍

author: @DerekRoss1958
text:
Very sensible!

Fiat money has value because we must use it to pay debts that we owe to the government that issues it. That is why we use different currencies in different countries: because nearly all the citizens and organisations within a country owe taxes to that country's government. The problem with a cryptocurrency is that there is no one insisting that anyone use that cryptocurrency to pay debts that people owe to them. And a fiat currency that can't be used to pay debts doesn't have a clear value. So we can never trust that it should have any particular value. And therefore no one knows what value it will have. Hence the wild changes in value for many cryptocurrencies.

If there were enough loans in a cryptocurrency that people needed that cryptocurrency to repay the loans, or a government accepted it in payment of taxes, it would give a more stable value to that cryptocurrency. But I don't think that many people want to borrow cryptocurrencies at the moment, let alone pay taxes in them! So it's better to stick to ordinary money.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Turn off the electricity and all digital forms of money [crypto or fiat debt] disappear.

Paper currencies are only more faith based because you can actually see or hold something.

But paper can be damaged by rotting fire, flood, forgery or even theft.

Metal money, is heavy, has a technical limit on availability [more theoretical than real], can be 'defaced', diluted, corroded, melted, and  [in a sense], forged.

Pure, solid metal, can still be diluted, forged, and can be quite heavy to carry.

Tally sticks can be burned, rotted, broken or stolen, but they can not be forged, diluted, or stolen [because the king holds half of every tally stick and they can only be transferred with the king's consent].

author: @Istandby666
text:
Why crypto will have value?
When a country stops printing money and moves to a crypto like currency. Countries will save money on paper, on security, and the cost of making the plates.
It would take about 25 years for paper/ coin currency to disappear after moving from it.
Looking towards the future. To go green. Countries will have to stop printing money and the next best thing will be a form of cryptocurrency.
I got involved in crypto back when you had to write the software and it used your graphics card to mine for Bitcoin.
I've had fun with the money I've made.
But, yes, a lot of crypto accounts will try and spam you out of your money for their personal gain.
You might be better off getting involved with crypto through a stock exchange.

replies.author: @RoddyRoams
replies.text: No country would want to use another crypto when it's likely to be more secure to create their own? Using an unregulated coin gives power to the unscrupulous who have already been able to amass the crypto "wealth".

replies.author: @MisterkeTube
replies.text: Countries would likely use centrally-controlled digital currency rather than crypto.  Crypto is just a Ponzischeme - some also wasting loads of energy, but nothing more ...

author: @ibme8359
text: There's a saying ("Figures don't lie, but liers can figure")! I for one tend to keep with tangible assets whenever possible, don't like any of that "funny money"! Gotta dig a few holes and hide it here and there, plus keep a treasure map (coded of course). That's my solution!!!
